title: Anupam Manur
text: I am a Professor of Economics at the Takshashila Institution, where I research on various facets of economic policy. I’m also the Managing Editor of the Indian Public Policy Review and co-author of We, the Citizens, a graphic novel on public policy. My personal claim to fame is that I can make almost anyone like economics!

title: Anupam Manur
text: I am a Professor of Economics at the Takshashila Institution, where I research on various facets of economic policy. I’m also the Managing Editor of the Indian Public Policy Review and co-author of We, the Citizens, a graphic novel on public policy. My personal claim to fame is that I can make almost anyone like economics!

title: Anupam Manur
text:
What I Do


Research & Writing
I write about economic policy, trade, platform economics, and bioeconomy. My work appears in various newspapers and other publications.


Teaching
I teach economics and public policy at the Takshashila Institution.


Indian Public Policy Review
I am the Managing Editor of IPPR, a peer-reviewed, open-access journal on Indian public policy.


Books
I co-authored We, the Citizens, a graphic novel that decodes public policy in the Indian context.



  
    
    Search posts, op-eds, publications…

title: Books
text:
We, the Citizens (2024)




Co-authored with: Pranay Kotasthane and Khyati Pathak
Penguin Random House India, 2024
What is a republic? How do markets work? What is the role of society in bringing about change? Many of us are unaware of what these entities stand for, how they interact with each other, and how they touch our lives.
We, The Citizens decodes public policy in the Indian context in a graphical narrative format relatable to readers of all ages. Whether you want to become an engaged citizen, aspire to be a positive change-maker, or wish to understand our sociopolitical environment, this book is for you.
The idea of India was an audacious dream. The fulfilment of this dream lies upon We, the citizens.
Buy on Amazon →




A peek inside — panels from the book:






The state and the market are not the only change-makers — society is too







This dichotomy assumes solutions lie either with the state or the market









State intervention does not come cheap — the marginal cost of public funds







It’s a Sisyphean Marathon









Policies that were once acceptable may become unacceptable as society shifts

title: Courses
text: I teach courses at the Takshashila Institution on economics and public policy.

title: Courses
text:
Current Modules

Economic Reasoning for Public Policy
Applying the concepts of economics to analyse and formulate public policy.
Course: GCPP, PGP


Information Economics
A module on the economics of information goods, with a special focus on platform economics.
Course: GCPP - Tech Policy


Strategic Economics
A module on understanding economics statecraft.
Course: GCPP - Defence & Foreign Affairs


Policy Pathways
A course on fundamentals of economics and public policy to 8th & 9th graders.
Course: GenWise Wizards


Nagara
I conduct a 3-hour long role-playing, democracy simulation game called Nagara, which is not only immensely enjoyable by participants, but is also a great teaching device to talk about government, policy, society and markets.

title: Courses
text:
Guest Lectures
I’m available for guest lectures on Economic Policy, Trade and Geoeconomics, Platform Economics, Bioeconomy, and to conduct Nagara. Contact me for speaking engagements.

title: Indian Public Policy Review
text: I am the Managing Editor of the Indian Public Policy Review — a peer-reviewed, open-access journal dedicated to advancing the understanding of public policy in India.

title: Indian Public Policy Review
text:
Current Issue — Vol. 7 No. 1 (2026)

  
    
  

This edition features five contributions examining diverse policy challenges. It begins with a conceptual piece on regulatory reform in consumer goods, followed by a macroeconomic analysis of employment disparities and their growth implications. The issue then presents a geographical analysis of Delhi’s stray dog crisis and associated rabies risks, a commentary proposing the expansion of India’s ecological fiscal transfer framework beyond forestry, and concludes with a critical review of recent scholarship on India’s post-independence developmental journey.
Read the full issue →
